title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (6)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored from the printed page, and clear OCR word-break, spacing, and punctuation errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Every day, Tamil Nadu is beset by death, murder, robbery, waylaying, chain-snatching, and the collapse of law and order. Over the past week in particular, heavy rain has been pouring down. Crops across several lakh acres lie submerged. Reports say that even the present Chief Minister, Panneerselvam, was held up by the rain and had to remain in his car for thirty minutes. No flood-relief work is under way. There is no need even to write about law and order. A minister's own brother has been murdered. Near Tiruvadanai in Ramanathapuram district, a young man taken to a police station for questioning is reported to have died after being assaulted by a sub-inspector. It has further been reported that, to cover this up, the same sub-inspector shot him and claimed that he had fired in self-defence.

At Neyveli, contract workers had been on strike for a month and a half. Only last night were they persuaded to withdraw it with a promise of some wage increase. There appears to have been no assurance about making the contract workers permanent. Though both the High Court and the Supreme Court ordered that the Sagayam inquiry commission begin its work, the AIADMK Government has shown no respect for those court orders. One report says that the Traders' Welfare Board has remained paralysed for three years. Is that the only board lying paralysed? Every welfare board for unorganised workers is paralysed. Tamil Nadu itself has been brought to a standstill.

Why, then, have I set all this aside and spent the past week writing about the judgment in Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case? Because the charge against Jayalalithaa, who had been Chief Minister, was proved; she was punished and lost her office. The first and fundamental cause was none other than herself. They themselves sowed the seed of their fate. At the same time, the people of Tamil Nadu must understand clearly what the judgment actually says.

In yesterday's letter I wrote, “When a woman is sentenced to prison, it is only natural for mothers to feel sympathy.” If the law is common to all, it must be common to both men and women. If a woman fails to conduct herself properly, becomes captive to greed, and misuses the governing authority entrusted to her by the people, the law cannot bend merely because she is a woman. Though this case and judgment contain an ocean of facts that could be explained at still greater length, only a mustard seed's worth has found room in this series.
One should not imagine that this is the first such judgment delivered against Jayalalithaa. Let me briefly recall what judges had already said about her in other cases.

On 24 November 2003, when Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister, Supreme Court Justices Rajendra Babu and P. V. Reddy observed in their judgment in the TANSI case: “Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has gone to the extent of claiming that even her own signature is not hers. Jayalalithaa must act according to her conscience and seek to make amends. Those who hold high office, such as the Chief Minister, must respect the rules. Just as an ordinary government employee cannot purchase government property, it is wrong for a person holding the office of Chief Minister to attempt to purchase the property of a government undertaking. The law is the same for an ordinary government employee and a person in high office. She must regret the wrong and return the land she purchased to TANSI.” Can that be forgotten?

In the income-tax return case concerning Jayalalithaa and Sasikala for 1993-94, Supreme Court Justices Agarwal and Mathur said in their judgment of 24 February 2006: “You are making a mockery of the judicial process. How long can you drag the proceedings? You should be a model to others. What counter-affidavit can you possibly file in this matter?” There is that precedent too.

During the very period in which the disproportionate assets were accumulated, 1991 to 1996, a case was filed against then Chief Minister Jayalalithaa for granting permission to the Pleasant Stay Hotel in Kodaikanal in violation of the rules. She was sentenced to one year in prison. AIADMK members responded then too with violence. Near Dharmapuri they set fire to a bus carrying students of the Coimbatore Agricultural College, burning three young women to death. When the prosecution delayed that case, was the judgment delivered by High Court Justice Kanagaraj a certificate of praise for the AIADMK Government?

When this very disproportionate-assets case was transferred to Karnataka, I quoted at the beginning of this series the words used by Supreme Court Justices S. N. Variava and H. K. Sema. Were those ordinary words?
The Bengaluru Special Court's judgment now joins this list of judgments concerning Jayalalithaa.

Yesterday I wrote about how much jewellery Jayalalithaa owned at the outset, before she became Chief Minister in 1991, and how many times it had multiplied by 1996. Yet on 30 July 1990, she issued a half-page statement in the daily Maalai Malar. In it she said: “After the election, I fulfilled my duty to return the money given by the cadres. To safeguard the trust placed in me by the party's Udanpirappugal, I sold my own jewellery, sold the antique jewellery and silverware left by my ancestors, mortgaged my property, and borrowed from a few friends to refund the deposits in full.” Compare her 1990 declaration that she had sold all her jewellery with the amount she declared in 1991 and the number of jewels she possessed by 1996. The truth becomes perfectly clear.

In the disproportionate-assets case, the central question was whether she had acquired assets worth Rs 66.65 crore beyond her lawful income. In his judgment, Judge Cunha wrote that assets worth Rs 55.79 crore held in the names of Jayalalithaa and her benamis had been proved beyond doubt to be in excess of lawful income, that the accused could provide no proper account for them, and that Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi were conspirators in the accumulation of assets. It had also been proved beyond doubt that the others assisted Jayalalithaa in accumulating them.

Lakhs of rupees obtained unlawfully and left unexplained were credited to the accounts of companies in which the accused were partners, while vast amounts of property were accumulated. Three thousand acres of land were purchased. Consider just one example of the irregular credits. Arunachalam, manager of Indian Bank's Abhiramapuram branch in Chennai, testified that the account of Riverway Agro Products, one of the accused companies, received Rs 15.45 lakh on 29 November 1994, Rs 19.50 lakh on 30 November, Rs 22.41 lakh on 3 December, Rs 15 lakh on 7 January 1995, Rs 25 lakh on 10 January, another Rs 25 lakh on 12 January, Rs 19 lakh on 25 April, Rs 20 lakh on 27 April, and Rs 19.90 lakh on 28 April. If lakhs were deposited every day or every other day, where did this money pour from, and how did it accumulate? Page 752 of Judge Cunha's judgment shows that the judge himself was struck by this question.

The judge also clearly recorded, through the testimony of many witnesses, the accumulation of land. Here is one example of its scale: 53 acres on 22 August 1994; 281 acres on 17 November 1994; 507 acres on 22 December 1994; 166 acres on 6 January 1995; and 158 acres on 21 February 1995. The list goes on. On 17 November 1994 alone, purchases were successively made of 281 acres, 73.90 acres, 69.78 acres, 60.65 acres, 42.31 acres, and 34.815 acres. When the Special Court asks in astonishment where the money came from to buy and accumulate so many acres so freely, should those virtuous people who put up posters accusing the very court not use their powers of reason?

I have already discussed the expenditure accounts presented by Jayalalithaa's side, especially the expenditure on Sudhakaran's wedding and who actually met those costs. Let me similarly offer a few examples of how some of the claimed income was said to have arisen. Jaya Publications, owned by Jayalalithaa and Sasikala, leased a building in Chennai's Guindy Industrial Estate from SPIC for three years, from 1993 to 1996, sublet it to another company, and claimed rental income of Rs 37.67 lakh. Likewise, they claimed lease income of Rs 30.40 lakh by leasing to SPIC a site in Cuddalore's SIPCOT Industrial Estate belonging to Indo Doha Chemicals.

Jayalalithaa's side sought to add another Rs 4.29 crore to her stated income. This comprised Rs 2.15 crore in gifts from party members for her forty-fourth birthday, Rs 78 lakh in gifts received from abroad, Rs 78 lakh in interest income, and Rs 47 lakh in income from a grape farm. Sasikala similarly sought to add Rs 19.28 crore to her income, including Rs 14.01 crore claimed through the newspaper Namadhu MGR and Rs 52 lakh received from abroad.
The prosecution had already calculated their income during the period under scrutiny at Rs 9.34 crore. They sought to add another Rs 24.9 crore and place their total income at Rs 34.24 crore.

As for the birthday gifts, cross-examination showed that those presented as party office-bearers had no proof that they held the positions claimed; the party had passed no resolution to collect such funds; cadres received no receipts for their contributions; and there was no acknowledgement that demand drafts had been received at party headquarters. An auditor named Shanmugasundaram testified for Jayalalithaa that every demand draft received as a birthday gift had been paid into her bank account and included in her income-tax return. Cross-examination, however, revealed that he was not even Jayalalithaa's auditor when this happened.

The Commissioner of Income Tax further stated: “Details of these gifts were not disclosed in the relevant years. They were disclosed only several years later. Even then, up to 1991 she had stated that she received only jewellery as gifts. Apart from her own claim, there is no evidence that she received money as gifts.”

After discussing these gifts in detail, Judge Cunha concluded: “Birthday gifts are not unlawful in themselves. But when the accused received such a large sum after assuming office as Chief Minister, it raises doubt about the source of the funds. Further, as the opposing side asks, if receiving such gifts was customary, why were none received after 1992? Even if we accept the argument that the money was a gift, it was still unlawful for Jayalalithaa to receive it while serving as Chief Minister.”

The judgment also cited the Supreme Court's reasoning: if public servants, who are prohibited from taking bribes, are allowed to accept gifts, bribes will be accepted in the form of gifts. Under Section 165 of the Indian Penal Code, a gift or bribe need not be received for a specified favour. Gifts received without a stated purpose are also prohibited because they can induce a public servant to act favourably towards the giver. Judge Cunha therefore held that money received as gifts could not be treated as lawful income.

On Sasikala's request to add Rs 19,28,80,000 to her income, including Rs 14.01 crore allegedly earned through Namadhu MGR, Judge Cunha said that neither Jayalalithaa nor Sasikala supplied the Income Tax Department with any documents concerning the newspaper's deposit scheme during its inquiry. When the department seized Namadhu MGR's account books, those books too contained no entries relating to receipts under such a scheme.

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala, he said, created the story of this scheme in their income-tax filings only after the charge sheet was filed against them in 1998. Even up to 2001, they told the department that the applications and receipt copies connected with the scheme had been lost and that a police complaint had been filed. Yet, astonishingly, in 2012 they themselves submitted the originals of those “lost” documents, claiming to have obtained them from the Income Tax Department.

The documents themselves revealed that they had been created recently. To make them look old, the sheets had been exposed to smoke or left in the sun, while the writing on them remained fresh and bright. They also lacked basic information, including receipt numbers. These features, the judge stated plainly, proved the documents to be fabricated.

When property is purchased in a company's name, the company's Common Seal must be affixed to the sale deed. Judge Cunha pointed out that none of the sale deeds for properties purchased by the companies involved in this case bore the respective company's Common Seal. Nor did the deeds state the companies' proper addresses. He explained that these companies had thus been formed, and properties purchased, through unlawful means.

No one who understands the law can question the foundation of the disproportionate-assets corruption case against Jayalalithaa and her associates. At the very outset, when Jayalalithaa sought anticipatory bail, the Madras High Court rejected it, holding that there was prima facie evidence for the corruption case and that the claim that it had been brought out of political animosity was incorrect. That judgment cannot be forgotten.

After the Bengaluru Special Court's judgment sent Jayalalithaa and the others to prison, the country watched in shock as AIADMK members deliberately degraded the judgment, the court and the judge in unprintable language. They knew that a court judgment must be challenged legally through the courts, not by taking to the street, abandoning civility, and pouring out fourth-rate abuse with unrestrained tongues. Tamil Nadu's people were forced to endure the continuing frenzy of violence, displays of official arrogance, disruption of public peace, destruction of public property, and ruling-party scenes that treated law and order as worth less than a farthing, while police officers duty-bound to protect the public stood inactive and watched.

Disregarding and insulting a court judgment, then attempting to secure a remedy through street protests, may gratify a few people but can never produce a meaningful result. Shouting from the roadside about a false case, conspiracy and deceit will not change a court's verdict. Everyone must heed the Supreme Court's warning: “However great you may be, the law is greater than you.” The rule of law includes the courts. To revile a court judgment is therefore like breaking one leg of the chair of democracy on which we sit in safety and peace.

Apart from what I have explained about the Bengaluru Special Court judgment, let us see what some publications said. In an editorial titled “The Lesson of the Judgment,” Ananda Vikatan wrote: “This is certainly a momentous judgment. Justice has been upheld; confidence in the courts has been reaffirmed. The Bengaluru Special Court's judgment is of exceptional importance in India's political history.”

In its editorial of 2 October 2014, The Times of India wrote that if the punishment was considered unjust, Indian law provided ample avenues of appeal. The leadership of the AIADMK, the ruling party in Tamil Nadu, should properly advise its cadres to abide by the outcomes of the law.

The Hindu reported that even the transfer of Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case to Karnataka in 2003 occurred because the Supreme Court concluded that an impartial and fair trial would not be possible in Tamil Nadu and transferred it to ensure justice.

Writing in The Hindu, Supreme Court advocate Sanjay Hegde said: “From the Prime Minister to an ordinary policeman, from a cobbler to an emperor, the law is the same for everyone. However great you may be, you must understand that the law is always above you.”

In an article in Frontline, T. S. Subramanian wrote: “To ensure that no one could allege malicious intent, the DMK Government clearly instructed the investigating officers not to overvalue the properties of Jayalalithaa and the other accused. That is what strengthened the case.”

Udanpirappē, before I bring this long series of letters to an end with a heavy heart, I bow to Bengaluru Special Court Judge Cunha for adhering to justice and fearlessly delivering a judgment in the spirit of “Truth Alone Triumphs.” I offer my abundant thanks and appreciation to the DMK advocates Shanmugasundaram, Kumaresan, Saravanan, Thamaraiselvan, Balaji and Natesan, who worked to bring this judgment to light; to the investigating officers Nallamma Naidu and Thukkaiyandi, who discharged their duty conscientiously; and to Acharya and Murugesh Maradi, who served ably as public prosecutors. With that, I conclude this series.
With affection,
M.K.
